title: Slavic Folk Magic and Curse-Lifting as Anger Work
dek: When the body holds a curse, it is not a metaphor. It is a knot in the fascia, a tightening of the jaw, a coldness in the gut. Slavic folk magic understands that anger is the fire required to burn the curse away, returning the body to its sovereign state.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Anatomy of a Curse


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.229Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You wake up at 3:14 AM, and the right side of your neck is rigid. The levator scapulae is screaming, pulling your shoulder up toward your ear as if bracing for a blow that has already landed. You might call it stress. You might call it a bad sleeping position. But if you trace the tension down into the belly of the muscle, if you feel the cold, hard knot that refuses to yield to massage or heat, you might recognize it for what it is: a curse.

In the modern, sanitized world, we do not like the word curse. We prefer clinical terms. We talk about trauma, about somatic holding patterns, about the sympathetic nervous system locked in fight or flight. And all of that is true. As [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) has taught us so thoroughly, the body keeps the score. The trauma is not just in the mind; it is in the tissues, in the viscera, in the very way we breathe. But the old women of Eastern Europe, the babushkas and the whisperers, they had another language for this. They understood that when someone directs malice toward you, when a system crushes you, when you are forced to swallow your own fury to survive, that energy does not just vanish. It lodges in the body. It becomes a curse.

## The Fire of Refusal

Anger is not a moral failure. It is not a low-vibrational emotion that you need to meditate away. Anger is the body's immune response to violation. When you are cursed - whether by a cruel word from a parent, a systemic injustice, or a literal hex - your body knows before your mind does. The jaw clenches. The masseter muscle tightens so hard you grind your teeth to dust in your sleep. The psoas contracts, preparing you to run or fight, but you do neither. You sit still. You smile. You swallow the fire.

This swallowed fire turns inward. It burns the stomach lining. It creates stagnation in the liver. In [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20), anger is associated with the Liver meridian, and when it is thwarted, it causes Liver Qi stagnation. You feel it as a lump in the throat, a tightness in the chest, a bitter taste in the mouth. The curse is not just the original violation; it is the ongoing suppression of your own life force.

[FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) writes extensively about how the suppression of healthy anger leads to autoimmune disease, to the body attacking itself. When we cannot say no to the outside world, our bodies say no for us, often in the form of illness. The curse takes root in the physical form because we have been taught that our anger is dangerous, ugly, and unspiritual.

## The Whisperers and the Wax

In Slavic folk magic, curse-lifting is a somatic practice. It is not about positive thinking. It is about moving energy out of the physical body. One of the most common practices is wax pouring (ceromancy). The practitioner melts beeswax and pours it into a bowl of cold water held over the head of the afflicted person. As the wax hits the water, it hisses and spits, solidifying into shapes that reveal the nature of the curse.

But the magic is not just in the wax. It is in the whisperer, the *szeptucha*, who mutters prayers and incantations, demanding that the sickness, the evil eye, the curse leave the body. She speaks to the bones, to the blood, to the breath. She uses herbs - mugwort, nettle, St. John's wort - not as gentle teas, but as fierce allies. Nettle stings. It wakes up the stagnant blood. It demands attention. When you drink a strong infusion of nettle, you are drinking the earth's own boundaries.

This is where the anger comes in. To lift a curse, you must be willing to get angry. You must be willing to say, *This does not belong to me. I return it to the earth. I return it to the sender.* You cannot politely ask a curse to leave. You must evict it. You must use the fire of your anger to burn it out of your fascia.

## The Somatics of Eviction

Let us talk about the diaphragm. The diaphragm is the great muscle of respiration, sitting like a parachute beneath your lungs. When you are frightened, when you are cursed, the diaphragm freezes. You take shallow, chest breaths. You do not let the air reach your belly, because if you did, you might feel the terror and the rage stored there.

Resmaa Menakem, in his vital work on somatic abolitionism, teaches us that trauma is decontextualized in the body over time. It becomes the water we swim in. We don't even realize our diaphragm is frozen until we try to take a deep breath and find we cannot. The curse of systemic oppression, of generational trauma, lives in the constriction of our breath.

To lift this curse, we must thaw the diaphragm. We must allow the breath to descend into the belly, and we must allow the sounds of our anger to rise up. Not screaming at a person, but the primal, guttural sound of the body reclaiming its space. It is the sound of the bear waking up from hibernation. It is the sound of the earth cracking open.

## A Practice of Return

Here is a short embodied practice for lifting the curse of swallowed anger. Do this at night, when the house is quiet. You will need a bowl of cold water and a match.

1. Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Feel the floor beneath you. Notice where your body is holding tension. Is it the jaw? The shoulders? The gut?
2. Take a breath into that tension. Do not try to relax it. Just acknowledge it. Say to it, *I feel you holding the curse.*
3. Light the match. Watch the flame. Let it represent the fire of your anger, the healthy, protective fire that says *No.*
4. As the flame burns down, speak aloud what you are returning. *I return the shame. I return the silence. I return the belief that I am too much.*
5. Drop the match into the cold water. Hear the hiss. That is the sound of the curse breaking.
6. Shake your body. Start with your hands, then your arms, your shoulders, your hips, your legs. Shake violently. Let the fascia release. Let the animal of your body shake off the predator.

## The Medicine of Boundaries

When you lift a curse, you create a void. The space in your body that was occupied by the tension, the fear, the swallowed anger, is now empty. If you do not fill it, the curse will return. You must fill it with your own sovereign presence. You must fill it with boundaries.

Boundaries are not just words you say. They are a physical stance. They are the way you hold your spine. They are the way you meet someone's eyes. When your psoas is relaxed but ready, when your jaw is soft but firm, you are embodying a boundary. You are saying, *I am here, and you may not cross this line.*

Herbs can support this. Yarrow is the great boundary herb. It stops bleeding, both physical and energetic. It knits the edges of the aura back together. A tincture of yarrow, taken when you feel your boundaries slipping, reminds the body of its own integrity. It is a fierce, bitter medicine, and it works.

## The Alchemy of Rage

We have been taught to fear our rage, to see it as destructive. But in the context of curse-lifting, rage is alchemical. It is the heat required to melt the lead of our trauma into the gold of our sovereignty. When you allow yourself to feel the anger of having been violated, of having been silenced, you are not destroying yourself. You are destroying the curse.

Think of the Slavic witch, Baba Yaga. She is not a sweet fairy godmother. She is terrifying. She lives in a house on chicken legs, surrounded by a fence of glowing skulls. She is the embodiment of the wild, untamed, unapologetic feminine. She does not care if you like her. She cares if you are true.

When we do this anger work, we are calling on the energy of Baba Yaga. We are building our own fence of glowing skulls. We are saying that our bodies are sacred territory, and any trespass will be met with the fierce, protective fire of our own life force.

The body remembers everything. The fascia, that continuous web of connective tissue that wraps every muscle, bone, and organ, is like a recording device. It records the impact of every harsh word, every betrayal, every time you had to make yourself small to survive. When you try to stretch it out in a yoga class, it resists. It says, *No, we need this armor. It is not safe yet.* And it is right. You cannot force the fascia to release. You must convince it that the war is over. You must show it that you are now capable of defending yourself, that your anger is online and ready to protect you. Only then will the tissue begin to melt. Only then will the curse begin to lift. You feel it as a sudden warmth spreading down your arms, a tingling in your fingers, a deep, involuntary sigh that shudders through your entire chest cavity. This is the biology of liberation.

Consider the times of day when the curse feels heaviest. For many, it is the twilight hours, the transition between day and night, when the distractions of the world fall away and the body is left alone with its own sensations. The liver, according to the Chinese organ clock, is most active between 1 AM and 3 AM. If you are waking up consistently at this time, your body is trying to process the anger you swallowed during the day. It is trying to metabolize the poison. You can support this process not by taking a sleeping pill and forcing the body back into unconsciousness, but by getting up, placing your feet on the cold floor, and acknowledging the fire. Drink a cup of dandelion root tea. Dandelion is a weed that pushes through concrete. It is the perfect medicine for a liver burdened by the curse of compliance. It says, *I will grow here, no matter what you put on top of me.*

We must also speak of the throat. The throat chakra, the cervical spine, the vocal cords. How many times have you swallowed a scream? How many times have you smiled when you wanted to bare your teeth? The muscles of the throat - the sternocleidomastoid, the scalenes - become rigid with the effort of holding back the truth. This is a self-imposed curse, born of necessity. To lift it, you must use your voice. You must make sounds that are not pretty. You must groan, growl, hum, and wail. You must let the vibration of your own voice break up the stagnation in your throat. This is not about communicating with anyone else. It is about communicating with your own tissues. It is about telling your body, *I am allowed to take up space. I am allowed to make noise.*

The work of lifting a curse is not a one-time event. It is a practice. It is a daily commitment to your own sovereignty. It is the willingness to feel the discomfort of your own anger, rather than the familiar numbness of dissociation. When you choose to feel, you are choosing life. You are choosing to inhabit your body fully, with all its messy, fierce, and beautiful sensations. You are reclaiming your territory from the ghosts of the past, from the systemic forces that want you small and quiet. You are becoming your own healer, your own witch, your own protector.

Let us return to the image of the wax pouring. The wax solidifies into the shape of the fear, the shape of the curse. Once it is seen, it can be dealt with. The whisperer might bury the wax at a crossroads, or burn it in the fire. The ritual gives the mind a symbolic action to match the somatic release. You can create your own rituals. You can write the names of your violators on a piece of paper and burn it, watching the smoke carry the energy away. You can take a salt bath, scrubbing your skin with coarse sea salt, visualizing the curse being washed down the drain. The specific action matters less than the intention behind it. The intention must be absolute refusal. *I refuse to carry this anymore.*

This refusal is the core of anger work. It is the boundary that says, *This far, and no further.* It is the recognition that your body is not a dumping ground for other people's unhealed trauma. You are not a sponge. You are a sovereign being, with the right to defend your own borders. When you embody this refusal, your posture changes. Your chest opens. Your gaze becomes steady. You are no longer a target. You are a force of nature.

And what of the aftermath? When the curse is lifted, when the anger has burned through the stagnation, what is left? What is left is clarity. What is left is a deep, abiding peace that is not dependent on external circumstances. It is the peace of knowing that you are capable of protecting yourself. It is the peace of a nervous system that has returned to baseline, not because it is ignoring the danger, but because it knows it can handle it. This is the true goal of anger work. Not to be angry all the time, but to be free.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel terrified of my own anger. What if I lose control?**
**A:** This is the most common fear, born from a culture that equates anger with violence. But violence is what happens when anger is suppressed until it explodes. Healthy anger is precise. It is a boundary, not a bomb. Start small. Notice the tiny irritations in your body - the slight tightening of the jaw when someone interrupts you. Acknowledge it. You don't have to act on it immediately, just feel it. The body needs to know you are listening before it will trust you with the bigger rage.

**Q: Can I really lift a curse by myself, or do I need a practitioner?**
**A:** You are the ultimate authority on your own body. While a skilled somatic practitioner or a traditional healer can hold space and offer techniques, the actual eviction of the energy must come from you. Your body, your fire, your refusal. The herbs, the wax, the rituals - they are tools to help you access your own innate power.

**Q: How do I know if it's a curse or just my own trauma?**
**A:** In this framework, the distinction is less important than the somatic reality. Whether the energy was directed at you intentionally by another person, absorbed from a toxic environment, or inherited through your lineage, it is living in your tissues *now*. The treatment is the same: acknowledge the intrusion, access the protective fire of your anger, and evict the energy from your body.

**Q: What if I can't feel my anger at all? I just feel numb.**
**A:** Numbness is a profound protective mechanism. It means the nervous system decided the anger was too dangerous to feel, so it shut down the sensation entirely. Do not force it. Respect the numbness. Work with gentle movement, with thawing the edges. Focus on grounding your feet, on feeling the boundaries of your skin. The anger is still there, preserved in the ice. It will thaw when it is safe enough to do so.

---

<section data-author="byline">
